summary: The AIM Problem Lists dataset contains 101 open problems from AIM workshops spanning 80+ mathematical topics. Currently all tracked problems show resolved issues with zero open issues, indicating proof-completion activity is concentrated in additive combinatorics and hypergraph Turán problems. Proof status counters (q1-q10) each register 100 issues, suggesting a uniform, possibly automated proof-tracking process across the corpus.
problems: All listed problems (aim_addcombapp series and aim_hypergraphturan_001) show 0 open / 1 resolved issues, so there is no active open-issue backlog to triage from the provided data.
Most resolved issues carry placeholder '(untitled)' proof titles (e.g., aim_addcombapp_0001, _0004, _0005, _001), indicating missing or incomplete metadata.
Proof status fields q1 through q10 uniformly report exactly 100 issues each, a suspicious flat distribution that may signal a counting artifact or stub data rather than genuine per-question progress.
Only 7 problems have detailed issue breakdowns despite 101 problems in the dataset, leaving ~94 problems with unreported activity status.
highlights: aim_addcombapp_0002 has a properly titled, resolved proof ('3APs in sets of small growth'), demonstrating a clean, completed problem record.
The additive combinatorics cluster (aim_addcombapp) shows the highest traction, with 6 of 7 detailed problems fully resolved.
Every detailed problem reached a resolved state with zero lingering open issues, indicating strong closure discipline where tracking exists.
aim_hypergraphturan_001 is resolved, showing progress is broadening beyond a single topic area.

suggested_todos.title: Backfill '(untitled)' proof titles for aim_addcombapp_0001, _0004, _0005, _001 to improve searchability
Investigate the uniform 100-issue counts in proof status fields q1-q10 to confirm whether they reflect real data or a stub artifact
Audit the ~94 problems lacking issue breakdowns and ingest their open/resolved status
Document the resolved proof for '3APs in sets of small growth' as a template for well-formed problem records
Identify near-complete problems in additive combinatorics for likely short-term resolution
